scope.target: CUST-001 semantic customization schema and governance-auditor-upgrader lifecycle
constraints: Identify capability, rule, or contract semantics before path evidence
Require requirement, ADR, or workflow evidence when applicable
Keep ordinary target truth outside the ledger unless it changes framework behavior
Never retire a customization without explicit owner reconciliation
non_goals: Invent target customizations for the source repository
Overwrite target-owned requirements, ADRs, workflows, runbooks, or policies

steps: Update governance, auditor, upgrader, and initialization contracts
Exercise legacy and governed target fixtures
validation: customization schema positive and negative fixtures
execution.last_completed_step: Implemented and validated the governance-owned customization lifecycle, atomic initialization, auditor verification, upgrader reconciliation, source/target validator split, and legacy unresolved conversion
execution.next_action: Use the completed lifecycle contract in the workflow-wide downstream package and final validation gates

results.summary: The v0.6 customization ledger now identifies capability, rule, or contract semantics before paths and requires base evidence, dependencies, owner reconciliation, decision evidence, baseline audit, incoming equivalence, post-upgrade audit, disposition, and validation. Governance owns the ledger, the auditor verifies active context, initialization writes both authorities atomically only from credible evidence, and the upgrader finalizes only after validation.
tests_run: 19 AI context version governance tests passed
5 four-skill and source/target projection contract tests passed
AI context validation passed for 24 indexes, 14 canonical skills, 307 language-policy files, and 32 manifests
shell asset validation passed and check-all.sh syntax passed under Git Bash
skill-creator quick validation passed for all four Agents runtime wrappers
Python compilation and git diff --check passed with expected CFG-001 line-ending warnings
results.residual_risk: A disposable lifecycle fixture proves the contract and rollback boundary, but a real downstream repository upgrade remains a release-candidate validation responsibility.
